content: This thread is for a continuation of this discussion on MO chat which was a continuation of the comments on this answer. Because Zulip is a better platform for conversations than either MO comments or MO chat.
Oh cool!
Something I've been meaning to mention in this area is that Charles Rezk has a draft up on his website investigating oo-categorical limit doctrines
https://faculty.math.illinois.edu/~rezk/accessible-cat-thoughts.pdf
The most exciting thing to me is that Charles discovered that oo-categorically, the doctrine of pullbacks is _sound_ (in the evident sense mimicking ABLR in oo-categories)
Notoriously, this doctrine is not sound 1-categorically.

That's neat that there's another way in which limits/colimits are better ∞\infty∞-categorically. I don't work enough with sound doctrines that it sounds exciting to me, though. Does it have useful applications?
I'm not sure! One thing it tells you is that Cat_oo (which is sketchable by a pullback sketch of course) has certain limit/colimit commmutativity properties.
PB-filteredness is equivalent, Charles shows, to being a space-indexed colimit of filtered categories
So colimits in Cat_oo indexed by such categories commute with pullbacks.
I think Charles gives some interesting unexpected examples like mapping tori

But I guess mostly I just find it suggestive -- in 1-categories, it seems to me the main limitation of sound doctrines is that there just don't seem to be that many of them, and you can really generally get by treating them on an ad hoc individual basis.
Maybe the true potential of the theory will be more fully realized in oo-categories.
∞\infty∞-categories

@Tim Campion:

I've had occasion to wonder recently if maybe the whole apparatus of limit doctrines could be redone with distributivity rather than commutation of limits and colimits.

To my understanding, the paper Accessibility and presentability in 2-categories by @Ivan Di Liberti and @fosco is very much inspired by this perspective.
@Nathanael Arkor Oh that's really cool! Could you explain more? I don't see the word "distribut-" appearing in the paper.
Perhaps the version on my computer is old though
The notion of "envelope" (Definition 3.1) can be rephrased in terms of a distributive law of KZ doctrines (for S over D), so that P is the composite SD.

How does one post here without replying to anybody? Anyway, Corollary 2.5 in Johnstone–Joyal (Continuous categories and exponentiable toposes) proves that locally finitely presentable categories are continuous, hence also precontinuous, which answers a question asked on the MO chat.
If you're viewing a topic, then your replies go to that topic; there is no notion of "replying" to an individual post other than to the topic it's in.

Mike Shulman said:

If you're viewing a topic, then your replies go to that topic; there is no notion of "replying" to an individual post other than to the topic it's in.

Apart from quoting :)
(Which can be done with the drop-down menu by clicking the three dots on the rightmost side of a post.)
Okay, so basically Zulip is a glorified IRC (if anybody still remembers what that is...).
Yeah, that was my reaction too. (-:
IRC didn't have math syntax, so that's something.
@Dmitri Pavlov Thanks for the reference. Taking a look, I _think_ the idea generalizes as follows: any KZ-doctrine distributes over the doctrine Lim
And a free algebra for the KZ doctrine is automatically an algebra for the composite doctrine
I think it just boils down to the fact that the structure map for the algebra fits into an appropriate string of adjoints (by virtue of KZ ness), and in particular preserves limits.

Tim Campion said:

Dmitri Pavlov Thanks for the reference. Taking a look, I _think_ the idea generalizes as follows: any KZ-doctrine distributes over the doctrine Lim

This is certainly true for D-Ind\mathbb D\text{-}\mathrm{Ind}D-Ind for sound D\mathbb DD.
(By Corollary 6.4 of A classification of accessible categories.)
Right... my claim does seem fishy, given that ABLR have to do _some_ work to prove there's a distributive law.
I suppose what I should really claim is that _if_ there's a distributive law, then the free algebras for the KZ monad are automatically algebras for the composite monad
In the particular case of composing the one's KZ doctrine with the doctrine Lim
Surely that can't be true unless your algebra is free on a Lim-algebra?
(in which case it should follow formally from the existence of a distributive law, right?)
I agree that case follows formally from the existence of a distributive law
Okay -- there's an additional requirement.
Let T be a KZ doctrine and suppose that T distributes over Lim. Let C be a category, and suppose that T(C) in fact has limits. Then T(C) is an algebra for T∘LimT \circ LimT∘Lim
Proof: the only thing to check is that the structure map T2(C)→T(C)T^2(C) \to T(C)T2(C)→T(C) preserves limits. But by KZness it has a left adjoint
The left adjoint is T(y)T(y)T(y) where yyy is the unit for the KZ doctrine

I would have to look up the definitions to verify that that particular adjunction is guaranteed to exist by the KZ property, but at least it seems to be the case when T=IndT = IndT=Ind (and this adjunction is the core of the Johnstone-Joyal proof).
So I feel pretty comfortable asserting that this is the correct generalization.
My memory of lax-idempotence is that yTy_TyT​ is right adjoint to the structure map. I don't remember anything about T(y)T(y)T(y) being left adjoint to it.
I think they both hold
You only get one adjunction for a generic T-algebra
But we have a free T-algebra
So both adjunctions potentially make sense
And I believe they both hold
Ah, that could be.
I think Marmolejo and Wood talk about this adjoint triple
Yes, it's on the first page of Kan extensions and lax idempotent pseudomonads.

Mike Shulman said:

If you're viewing a topic, then your replies go to that topic...

Unless of course you edit the topic or the stream to change it - which is what you do if you want to start a new discussion.

I've dipped my toe into using Discord for math -- there's an Algebraic Topology server (there's a standing invite at nodorek.net). Over there there's just one level of organization into "channels". But it seems convenient to have both "streams" and "threads".
or was it "streams" and "topics"
I see, yes, "topic"
_italics_
no
bold
yes
*italics*
italics
nice, thanks!
_italics_
yeah!
You can examine the source for someone's post using the dropdown menu, which helps to find out the syntax other people are using.
That last one was guessable :)
Let's see. Does this work in great generality?
Let K\mathcal KK be any class of small categories
To be used for indexing colimits
The free K\mathcal KK-cocompletion can always be computed as the closure in the presheaf category under K\mathcal KK-indexed colimits.
Call this functor PKP_{\mathcal K}PK​
Suppose that PK(C)P_{\mathcal K}(C)PK​(C) is naturally reflective in P(C)P(C)P(C) for any small CCC.
Where P(C)P(C)P(C) is the presheaf category.
Then for every small CCC, PK(C)P_{\mathcal K}(C)PK​(C) is complete.

And by the previous argment, since PKP_{\mathcal K}PK​ is lax-idempotent, the structure map PK2(C)→PK(C)P_{\mathcal K}^2(C) \to P_{\mathcal K}(C)PK2​(C)→PK​(C) has a left adjoint and so is continuous.
So in order to get a distributive law with LimLimLim, and have that PK(C)P_{\mathcal K}(C)PK​(C) is always an algebra for the composite monad, well, we just need the distributive law.
(I am still perpetually confused about which monad is said to distribute over which other one)
The thing to check is that PKP_{\mathcal K}PK​ preserves completeness of categories and continuity of functors between complete categories.

(It's good to remember that multiplication distributes over addition, so timesy things like the monad for cartesian categories tend to distribute over plussy things like the monad for categories with some class of colimits.)
(But you probably know that.)
@John Baez Ah, thanks. Yeah, that makes sense.

I think the part which confuses me is that to verify the distributive law of LimLimLim over PPP so that P∘LimP \circ LimP∘Lim is a monad, you need to consider Lim∘PLim \circ PLim∘P, which feels backwards
Of course, you just consider Lim∘PLim \circ PLim∘P in order to get a functor from it to P∘LimP \circ LimP∘Lim, but it still feels strange.
Er -- I guess I'm still a bit turned around :)

What I want to say is that PKP_{\mathcal K}PK​ will preserve right adjoints and that that will be enough to preserve continuity. Of course that doesn't quite work because we end up leaving adjoint-functor-theorem land.

I guess this is how I remember this stuff:

In a ring you can rewrite any product of sums as a sum of products (but not vice versa), so you define the monad for rings using a distributive law PS →\to→ SP, where P is for products (the monad for monoids) and S is for sums (the monad for abelian groups).
But then, yeah, the monad for rings is SP, with multiplication SPSP →\to→ SSPP →\to→ SP.
Thanks. I think I got that well enough to at least rewrite my confusion in an accurate way!
I managed not to be too confused by the fact that my PPP corresponds to your SSS :)
I think
However, LimLimLim is co-lax-idempotent.
So CCC is complete iff there is a right adjoint to the unit C→Lim(C)C \to Lim(C)C→Lim(C)
So completeness is witnessed by an adjunction. Unfortunately maybe that doesn't help.

Tim Campion said:

I managed not to be too confused by the fact that my PPP corresponds to your SSS :)

Darn, I was trying to confuse you.
It really feels like all that needs to be juggled is size. It doesn't feel like soundness should actually be necessary for anything here.
Let LκL_\kappaLκ​ be the doctrine for κ\kappaκ-small limits. If CCC is small, then LκCL_\kappa CLκ​C is small.
Let L=LimL = LimL=Lim be the dotrine for all small limits.
In general, the unit C→LκCC \to L_\kappa CC→Lκ​C induces PKC→PKLκCP_{\mathcal K} C \to P_{\mathcal K} L_\kappa CPK​C→PK​Lκ​C
If CCC is small, then LκCL_\kappa CLκ​C is small. By hypothesis, this implies that PKLκCP_{\mathcal K} L_\kappa CPK​Lκ​C is complete.
and in particular κ\kappaκ-complete.
By the universal property of LκL_\kappaLκ​, we get an induced functor LκPKC→PKLκCL_\kappa P_{\mathcal K} C \to P_{\mathcal K} L_\kappa CLκ​PK​C→PK​Lκ​C.
which hopefully gives us a distributive law.
Ah, but the hypothesis was a bit problematic.
Generally we expect that PKCP_{\mathcal K} CPK​C will only be complete, or even κ\kappaκ-complete, if CCC is appropriately cocomplete.
And that's where we might actually need some theory of limit doctrines to understand things.
Perhaps an easier approach would be: prove once and for all that there is a distributive law L∘P→P∘LL \circ P \to P \circ LL∘P→P∘L.
This is done in Day and Lack's "Limits of small functors"
in the enriched setting. So it seems to be reasonably formal.
Then all these other distributive laws are "sub-distributive laws" of that one.

I think it follows that there is a distributive law L∘PK→PK∘LL \circ P_{\mathcal K} \to P_{\mathcal K} \circ LL∘PK​→PK​∘L [which is a sub-distributive law of L∘P→P∘LL \circ P \to P \circ LL∘P→P∘L] if PKLCP_{\mathcal K} L CPK​LC is complete for every CCC.
And showing this does indeed seem to be the substantive part of any proof in the literature that there is such a distributive law for a particular K\mathcal KK.

Tim Campion said:

I think it follows that there is a distributive law L∘PK→PK∘LL \circ P_{\mathcal K} \to P_{\mathcal K} \circ LL∘PK​→PK​∘L [which is a sub-distributive law of L∘P→P∘LL \circ P \to P \circ LL∘P→P∘L] if PKLCP_{\mathcal K} L CPK​LC is complete for every CCC.

I think this is Theorem 5.4 of Karazeris–Velebil's Representability relative to a doctrine.
@Nathanael Arkor Thanks, that is very nice! And they are treating sub-monads of LLL at the same time!
I bet one can get away with this holding for CCC small.
Just because these monads should commute with colimits of OrdOrdOrd-indexed chains.
